text: The surgeon uses a Nathanson retractor to lift the omentum and transverse colon, revealing the transverse mesocolon and ligament of Treitz. A bulging low central pancreatic collection is visible through the mesocolon. Scissors are used to divide the ligament, avoiding injury to the small bowel. The thin mesocolon is entered, and suction is applied to remove liquid and semi-solid matter from the cavity. The harmonic scalpel enlarges the opening through the mesentery, allowing for minimally invasive necrosectomy to clear the walled-off pancreatic necrosis.

A bag is deployed into the abdominal cavity to collect solid necrotic pancreas pieces, reducing debris loss. Laparoscopic pledges and atraumatic graspers are used for necrosectomy. A gastroscope provides direct visualization of the cavity. The proximal jejunum is divided using an endoscopic stapler with a gold cartridge, and a mesenteric window is created with a harmonic scalpel. An enterotomy is made and anastomosed to the cavity using a continuous 3-0 PDS suture, ensuring adequate bites through both the cavity wall and bowel wall.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy by creating enterotomies in each limb of the small bowel and using an endoscopic stapler with a gold reload to create a long, wide anastomosis. The remaining enterotomy is closed with a three-oh PDS suture. Following the small bowel anastomosis, a cholecystectomy is performed. Calot's triangle is dissected, and the cystic duct is identified before conducting an on-table cholangiogram, which reveals two distal common bile duct filling defects. Laparoscopic common bile duct exploration is carried out using a choledochoscope to retrieve stones, and the choledochotomy is closed with a two-oh vicryl suture. The cystic duct is divided after applying a medium-sized Hem-o-lok proximally and a ligature clip distally, and the gallbladder is dissected from the gallbladder bed using diathermy.
